hero.subtitle: Preserving the Legacy of the Great Silk Road Through Digital Technologies
hero.description: An international research collaboration combining photogrammetry, AI-powered reconstruction, and immersive VR/AR to create high-fidelity digital twins of Turkmenistan's most significant Silk Road monuments — from the Parthian citadels of Nisa to the azure tilework of Seyitjemaleddin.

about.title: Documenting an Endangered Legacy
about.p1: The Amul Heritage Digital Initiative (AHDI) is an international research collaboration dedicated to documenting and digitally preserving UNESCO World Heritage sites along the Zarafshan-Karakum Corridor — the Turkmenistan section of the ancient Silk Road (Merv–Amul route).
about.p2: We employ cutting-edge photogrammetry, AI-powered 3D reconstruction, and immersive VR technologies to create high-fidelity digital twins of endangered archaeological monuments. These digital records serve as both scientific archives and educational resources for future generations.
about.p3: Our work bridges the disciplines of archaeology, computer science, and cultural heritage preservation, combining Chinese expertise in AI and machine learning with on-the-ground fieldwork expertise in Turkmenistan.

entry1.detail: First documentation project at Magtymguly Turkmen State University museums; artifact digitisation workflow established.

entry2.detail: Comprehensive 3D documentation of the Parthian capital; complex architectural survey completed.

entry3.detail: Three peer-reviewed papers published in CyberLeninka and eLibrary.ru; methodology formalised.

entry4.detail: High-resolution VR-capable model completed; international conference presentation at Altai State University.

entry5.detail: Planned documentation of the Merv oasis complex; expanded AI reconstruction pipeline.

methodology.title: Methodology
methodology.lead: We combine three complementary digital technologies to produce comprehensive, reproducible, and publicly accessible records of Silk Road heritage sites.

photogrammetry.title: Photogrammetry
photogrammetry.desc: High-precision 3D capture using both professional-grade equipment and accessible mobile applications such as Polycam. Thousands of overlapping photographs are processed through structure-from-motion algorithms to produce dense point clouds and textured meshes at millimetre accuracy. Field documentation is conducted in situ with minimal site disturbance.

ai.title: AI Reconstruction
ai.desc: Machine learning algorithms optimise and enhance raw photogrammetric outputs — automating texture processing, resolving occluded areas, and improving geometric fidelity. All AI-assisted outputs are clearly distinguished from primary photogrammetric records. Models are exported in open GLTF/GLB formats for maximum interoperability.

immersive.title: Immersive Experience
immersive.desc: Completed 3D models are deployed in VR/AR environments, enabling virtual site visits for researchers, educators, and the public worldwide. Interactive walkthroughs preserve the spatial experience of heritage sites for communities separated from them by distance, conflict, or restricted access.

projects.title: Research Projects
projects.lead: Each project produces a multi-layered digital archive: raw photogrammetric datasets, optimised 3D models, and immersive VR applications — all preserved in open formats.

card1.name: Archaeology & Geology Museum
card1.desc: Digital documentation of the museum collections at Magtymguly Turkmen State University — establishing the core photogrammetric workflow that has underpinned all subsequent AHDI projects.

card2.era: Parthian Era · 3rd Century BCE · Ahal Province
card2.name: Ancient Nisa Fortress
card2.desc: Comprehensive 3D documentation of the Parthian capital — one of the most significant archaeological sites in Central Asia. The project addressed the complex multi-period stratigraphy of this UNESCO World Heritage site.

card3.name: Seyitjemaleddin Mausoleum
card3.desc: A 15th-century architectural masterpiece of the Timurid period, renowned for its intricate glazed tilework. AHDI produced a high-resolution 3D model capturing original tile textures at sub-millimetre precision.

team.title: The People Behind the Work
team.lead: AHDI brings together specialists from Turkmenistan and China, combining deep local heritage knowledge with advanced AI and ML expertise.

contact.title: Contact
contact.description: We welcome correspondence from heritage researchers, archaeologists, conservators, and institutions with an interest in Central Asian heritage digitisation. We are particularly interested in collaborative opportunities related to the Merv archaeological complex and other Zarafshan-Karakum Corridor sites.
contact.focusValue:
Zarafshan-Karakum Corridor
UNESCO World Heritage · Merv–Amul Route
